reference_answer: Case No. SF-CE-2806-E; PERB Decision No. 2262.
Charging Party: Mutual Organization of Supervisors (MOS); Respondent: Fairfield-Suisun Unified School District.
For MOS: Rick Lemke, Job Steward. For the District: Littler Mendelson by Bruce Sarchet, Attorney.
Martinez (Chair), Dowdin Calvillo and Huguenin (Members); Member Huguenin authored the decision, and Member Dowdin Calvillo wrote a concurrence (Chair Martinez joined the decision).
Keith A. Campbell, hired as a school bus driver in or about 2001 and promoted to transportation supervisor in 2003.
EERA (Educational Employment Relations Act) section 3543.5(a), (b) and (c).
October 19, 2009.
November 12, 2009.
August 24, 2010.
January 25, 2011.
Campbell was terminated on March 25, 2010; interest runs from the date of termination (March 25, 2010).
(1) MOS files its charge (October 19, 2009); (2) the complaint issues (November 12, 2009); (3) the formal hearing (August 24, 2010); (4) the proposed decision (January 25, 2011).
"October 19, 2009" in the Procedural History, and "October 12, 2009" in the statute-of-limitations analysis (District Defenses). The two dates are inconsistent in the source.

(1) the employer took action to change policy; (2) the change concerns a matter within the scope of representation; (3) the action was taken without giving the exclusive representative notice or opportunity to bargain; (4) the action had a generalized effect or continuing impact. Walnut Valley Unified School District (1981) PERB Decision No. 160; Grant Joint Union High School District (1982) PERB Decision No. 196.
Verbal reprimand; Written reprimand; Discipline less than dismissal; Dismissal.

(1) where the unit member's conduct endangers the safety of students, employees, or District property (the Superintendent or designee may suspend with pay rather than follow the progressive steps); and (2) in cases of emergency, for reasons stated in writing and sent to MOS, the Superintendent or designee may skip one or more steps.
49 C.F.R. § 382 et seq. (specifically § 382.211).
District Administrative Regulation 4112.41, "Drug and Alcohol Testing For School Bus Drivers."

(1) establishing and enforcing a zero tolerance policy with respect to any MOS unit employee's alleged refusal to submit to a random controlled substance and/or alcohol test without meeting and negotiating with MOS; (2) interfering with the rights of employees to be represented by MOS; and (3) denying MOS its right to represent its unit members guaranteed by EERA.

Because waivers of the right to bargain are disfavored and must be shown by "clear and unmistakable" language, with the matter "fully discussed" and the union having "consciously yielded"; the safety-exception language of Article 11 did not effectuate such a waiver.

Because MOS first learned of the District's application of the zero tolerance provision to MOS unit members on May 15, 2009 (Campbell's termination notice), and filed its charge within six months of that date.

She found it unnecessary to decide whether Campbell's conduct fell within the Article 11 "safety" exception, because the District did not rely on Article 11 in terminating Campbell (it relied on the zero tolerance policy) and only claimed the safety exception after the charge was filed.

The Order (Part B.2) requires action within fifteen (15) days of service of the final decision; the narrative Remedy section instead says "within thirty (30) days of the effective date of this order." The two timeframes are inconsistent in the source.

Campbell was disciplined for refusing to comply with a directive from a testing technician (refusing to disrobe below the waist to permit inspection for a prosthetic device during a random drug test). The Board concluded this conduct fell outside both Article 11 exceptions: it did not threaten or expose to danger students, employees, or District property, and did not constitute an emergency.
